diagrams.title: StringJoiner Class Architecture
diagrams.description: A block diagram showing the internal structure of the StringJoiner class, its constructors, and methods
elements: Large rectangular box labeled 'StringJoiner Class' at the top
Two constructor boxes below: 'StringJoiner(CharSequence delimiter)' and 'StringJoiner(CharSequence delimiter, CharSequence prefix, CharSequence suffix)'
Horizontal row of method boxes: 'add(CharSequence newElement)', 'merge(StringJoiner other)', 'toString()', 'setEmptyValue(CharSequence emptyValue)'
Vertical arrows connecting constructors to methods showing 'creates instance' relationship
Small box at bottom showing 'implements CharSequence' connection
Labels: 'delimiter', 'prefix', 'suffix' shown as attributes inside the main class box
diagrams.purpose: Students understand the complete StringJoiner API including constructors with parameters and all available methods for manipulation

diagrams.title: StringJoiner Working Process Flow
diagrams.description: Step-by-step flow diagram showing how StringJoiner builds a string with delimiter, prefix, and suffix
elements: Start circle: 'Create StringJoiner(-, [ , ])'
First action box: 'add("Apple")' with arrow to state showing '[Apple]'
Second action box: 'add("Banana")' with arrow to state showing '[Apple-Banana]'
Third action box: 'add("Cherry")' with arrow to state showing '[Apple-Banana-Cherry]'
Final action box: 'toString()' with arrow to result box showing '[Apple-Banana-Cherry]'
Annotation boxes showing: 'delimiter=-', 'prefix=[', 'suffix=]' at each step
Dotted arrows showing how delimiter appears between elements, prefix wraps first, suffix wraps last
diagrams.purpose: Visualize the sequential process of building strings, understanding how delimiter separates elements and prefix/suffix wrap the final result

diagrams.title: String.join() Method Execution Flow
diagrams.description: Flowchart showing the internal execution of String.join() method when joining array elements
elements: Rectangular input box: 'String[] names = {"A", "B", "C"}'
Parallelogram: 'delimiter = ", "' parameter
Diamond decision shape: 'For each element in array'
Process box inside loop: 'Append element to result'
Decision diamond: 'More elements?' with Yes going back to loop, No proceeding
Process box: 'Insert delimiter between elements'
Final output parallelogram: 'Result: "A, B, C"'
Arrow labels showing 'First element: no delimiter' and 'Subsequent elements: add delimiter before'
diagrams.purpose: Understand how String.join() internally iterates through elements and strategically inserts delimiters only between elements (not after last)

diagrams.title: Performance Comparison: String Concatenation vs StringJoiner
diagrams.description: Side-by-side block diagram comparing memory allocation and object creation for both approaches
elements: Left side labeled 'Inefficient: + Operator in Loop': Three boxes showing 'String s1 = "A"', 'String s2 = s1 + "B" (new object)', 'String s3 = s2 + "C" (new object)'
Dotted outline around all three showing 'Creates 3 separate String objects'
Garbage collector icon with label 'Memory overhead, GC pressure'
Right side labeled 'Efficient: StringJoiner': Single box showing 'StringJoiner sj' with internal buffer
Arrow showing 'add("A")', 'add("B")', 'add("C")' modifying same internal buffer
Final arrow showing 'toString()' creating only ONE String object
Time complexity labels: Left side 'O(n²)', Right side 'O(n)'
Arrow from left to right labeled 'StringJoiner is 10-100x faster for large datasets'
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrate the significant performance advantage of StringJoiner over repeated concatenation, crucial for exam questions on optimization

diagrams.title: Real-World String Joining Use Cases
diagrams.description: Application diagram showing practical scenarios where string joining is essential in software development
elements: Four main application boxes arranged in quadrants
CSV Generation: 'String joiner = new StringJoiner(","); joiner.add(name1); joiner.add(name2);' with output 'name1,name2'
SQL IN Clause: 'StringJoiner("','"', '("', ')")' with output '(\'val1\',\'val2\',\'val3\')'
JSON Array: 'StringJoiner(", ", "[", "]")' with output '[item1, item2, item3]'
Log Formatting: 'StringJoiner(" | ", "INFO: ", "")' with output 'INFO: timestamp | message | level'
Connecting arrows from each use case to a central 'Output: Formatted String' box
diagrams.purpose: Connect theoretical knowledge to practical programming scenarios students will encounter in industry projects

charts.title: String Concatenation vs StringJoiner vs String.join() Comparison
charts.content: Three-column comparison table: Column 1 'String + Operator' shows syntax 'str1 + str2', performance 'O(n²) in loops', use case 'Simple 2-3 string joining', features 'No delimiter control, creates multiple objects'; Column 2 'StringJoiner Class' shows syntax 'new StringJoiner(delim, prefix, suffix)', performance 'O(n) always', use case 'Complex formatting with delimiters', features 'Configurable prefix/suffix, mutable, merge support'; Column 3 'String.join()' shows syntax 'String.join(delim, array/collection)', performance 'O(n)', use case 'Simple array/list joining', features 'Static method, no prefix/suffix, clean syntax'

charts.title: Decision Tree: Choosing String Joining Method
charts.content: Start diamond 'Need to join strings?' → Yes branch → Decision diamond 'Need custom prefix/suffix?' → Yes → Output 'Use StringJoiner class' → No branch → Decision diamond 'Working with Array/Collection?' → Yes → Output 'Use String.join()' → No branch → Decision diamond 'Just 2-3 strings?' → Yes → Output 'Use + operator' → No → Output 'Use StringJoiner for performance' → No branch from first decision → Output 'No joining needed'

charts.title: StringJoiner API Quick Reference
charts.content: Two-column table: Method column lists 'StringJoiner(CharSequence delim)', 'StringJoiner(CharSequence delim, CharSequence prefix, CharSequence suffix)', 'add(CharSequence element)', 'merge(StringJoiner other)', 'toString()', 'setEmptyValue(CharSequence value)'; Description column explains 'Creates with delimiter only', 'Creates with delimiter, prefix, suffix', 'Adds element with delimiter', 'Adds all elements from another StringJoiner without prefix', 'Returns complete string with prefix/suffix', 'Sets output when no elements added' with code examples for each

charts.title: Performance Characteristics Benchmark
charts.content: Four-row comparison table: Row 1 '10 elements' shows concatenation '0.1ms', StringJoiner '0.02ms', String.join '0.02ms'; Row 2 '100 elements' shows concatenation '1.5ms', StringJoiner '0.1ms', String.join '0.1ms'; Row 3 '1000 elements' shows concatenation '150ms', StringJoiner '1ms', String.join '1ms'; Row 4 'Memory Objects Created' shows concatenation 'n*(n+1)/2', StringJoiner '1', String.join '1' with conclusion 'StringJoiner and String.join are 10-100x faster for large data'

charts.title: StringJoiner add() vs merge() Method Flow
charts.content: Start box showing two StringJoiner instances 'sj1 = [A-B-C]' and 'sj2 = [D-E-F]' with delimiter '-' and prefix '[' suffix ']' → Decision diamond 'Which method?' → 'add()' path → Box 'sj1.add(sj2.toString())' → Result 'sj1 = [A-B-C-D-E-F]' with note 'Treats as single element, adds prefix+suffix of sj2 as content'; 'merge()' path → Box 'sj1.merge(sj2)' → Result 'sj1 = [A-B-C-D-E-F]' with note 'Removes prefix/suffix of sj2, adds elements individually' → Annotation 'merge() useful for combining groups'

branches: StringJoiner Class: Constructor options, add()/merge()/toString() methods, setEmptyValue(), delimiter/prefix/suffix configuration
String.join() Method: Static method, accepts delimiter + array/Iterable, no prefix/suffix support, clean one-liner syntax
Performance Optimization: Immutable strings cause O(n²) with + in loops, StringJoiner uses mutable internal buffer O(n), reduces GC overhead
Use Cases: CSV generation (comma delimiter), SQL IN clauses (single quotes + comma), JSON arrays (square brackets), logging (pipe separator), path building (File.separator)
Comparison with Alternatives: StringBuilder similar but no delimiter handling, StringBuffer is thread-safe but older, StringJoiner specialized for joining with delimiters
mindMaps.purpose: Comprehensive overview connecting all aspects of string joining: API methods, performance implications, practical applications, and how it compares to related utilities
